CURRENT AFFAIRS.

“ You will have lots to write about next wcgk,”
remarked & man in the procession on Thursday night.
He:is not & journalist or he would know that Thursday
night’s procession would not be l‘]'lll'(',h of a current
affair, that day week, The descriptive writers—and
some newspaper descriptive writers ought to be locked
up—have all let their adjectives fly and the reading
public hag had enough. But as we walked in the pro-
cession, anyway, we may as well make a little copy
out of it.  We had a preliminary parade of the stretch
between Grafton Street and Westland Row, and ulti-
mately decided when the appointed hour was noar af
hand to wait and see on. the pavement about half-way
down Nassau Street. A horse and man make a fine

-gight any time and anywhere, and the civie cavalry
that headed the procession looked well and did their
work well on Thursday night. By the time Asquith
appeared half-way down Nassau Street, it was plain to
anyone that his welcome was great and that it pro-

mised to be unique. At that time he appeared to be
standing hatless, busily engaged bowing his acknow-
ledgments. A lady in'the carriage—she looked very
young to be Mrs. Asquith, and we are not sure whether
she was Mrs, or Miss Asquith—appeared to be as
enthusiastic in dcknowledging the greetings as the
people were enthusiastic in giving them.

Whether it was the torches or not, our accustomed
coolness gave way by a few degrees, and wishing to
see more of the matter, we quietly slipped in amongst
some torch-bearers and marched with the procession
until we could march no further—that was to a point
in O’Connell Street, where we could see the speakers
but not hear them. In a window or two over
Jameson’s flower shop, in Nassau Street, a pack of

females were demonstrating in the Suffer interest—
we see that Messrs. Jameson repudiate all responsi-

bility for their presence.  They were booed “and
baned.  They were not worth so much attention.
e worst of booing under such circumstances is that
it sounds as a jarring note on a welcome, even though
the groans were for some of the gang of obnoxious

females. The waving of handkerchiefs from the ladies
in the windows along the route was a remarkable

demonstration; even over the offices of the ‘‘Irish
Times ’ the welcome was most enthusiastic. . How
is that ““ Alf’? By the way, talking of “ Alf,”” we
saw its editor, the ‘“ Saved ’’ Mr. Healy, who is also
anti-Trish Dublin Correspondent of Pigott’s ‘‘ Times,’”
in Nassau Street before the procession had passed. He
boarded a city-bound Sandymount tram; he no doubt
felt too limp for the short walk to Westmoreland
Street. %

It was a great welcome, and the main feature was
its spontaneity Of course, the torchmen and the
horsemen were organised, but in the main it was, on
the face of it, spontaneous. It must have been as a
red rag to a bull to the poor Mullah of Mallow and to
the *“ Trish Times.’* The Mullah’s paper, the * Freak

5, said: *“ He will be cheered by a paid crowd.”
Picture the great multitude on Thursday night, and
think of them as a ‘‘ paid crowd '’—it gives you an
insight into the wretched William’s mind; if William
s to pay his crowd, or lead them to expect payment,
that is his concern; but let us pass on, as we take little
no interest in that pervert Irishman now. The
sh Times’ was flabbergasted, its editor, the
Saved ‘‘ Healy,” is a person that is so narrow that a
very prominent and high-placed Irishman told us that
Squireen Mahaffy exerted a liberalising influence over
him!. The ‘“ Trish Times '"said: ‘ Certainly, it was
« well-organised reception.  Mr, Devlin had arranged
things exceedingly well, and, fortunately for them-
selves, the militant Suffragettes were prevented from
giving any trouble.’’

It was a greab reception, though it was rather in a
perfunctory manner that we lifted our hat, and we
cannot remember whether we cheered or not.
looked at the matter from the Irish point of view, and
forbunately or unfortunately, our head keeps working
when others appear to be overcome by excitement.
In our judgment it was well that Asquith came; and
coming it was ‘well that he got a most enthusiastic
reception. If a man in the crowd asked us ought he
cheer, we would reply: ‘‘ Yes; cheer like blazes,”” but
we would not be equal to that ourselves. The worst
of having a sense of humour and a working head is
thut you can see so many sides of a question at practi

We .»

cally the one time. . We did not forget, though we are
_sure tens of thiousands did, that as a mabber of fact
we have not yet got Home Rule. What did Asquith
ever do for Ireland?  As the past is past, we will not
dwell on the doors that he shut with a bang. If
Asquith is a statesman and a great man—and we don’t
know one-tenth enough about him to form any opinion
on the matter—he at most is a great Bnglishman,  We

. don’t believe in a ‘‘ Union of Hearts,” though we be-
lieve in a working and honourable treaty of peace
between Ireland and England; we don’t believe in
Britain and Ireland understanding each other, for that
seems to us impossible. There is an incompata-
bility of temper between Ireland and England, but
that is no reason that they shotld not be friends and
mutually help each other when they each live in their
own house. ‘We don’t want separation, and we want
to be friends with England—England after all is a
country where the people work hard for low wages,
and -why should we have permanent ill-will towards
the poor people—all we want, as the horizon is at pre-
sent, is that Ireland be allowed scope, as free as
practicable, to develop her own individuality. So
long as England does not unreasonably ~thwart us,
why should we be anything but friendly to her? Our
anxieties about Home Rule are not so much about its
imperfections as about the possibility that we may not
get it.  Asquith is out for Home Rule now, and he
made a fine, measured speech on Iriday night, and
his visit to Dublin and his unparalleled reception in
Dublin, obviously, have speeded on the cause.  After

_ such a reception Asquith must have new heart in the
enterprise, and such a reception must have great influ-
ence in England in favour of Home Rule. The
Asquith visit, taken all round, has, in our opinion,
been a signal help to the fight and everyone connected
with it—in a friendly way—are to be heartily congratu-
lated on its success.

Clery’s had flags out, or to be. accurate, the Hotel
over Clery’s shop, that is owned by William Martin
Murphy, displayed them. If we said that Clery’s
had flags it might produce a similar case to the flag
display over the ‘‘Independent” office, but which
in reality, as the ‘‘ rebel '’ paper explained, was over
a Government office. One of the flags out of Murphy’s
Hotel contained a Union Jack. When that emblem was
erroneously alleged to have been unfurled by Murphy’s
““ Independent *’ it was looked upon as a damaging
allegation, and was promptly repudiated and an
apology was greedily grabbed at! Murphy is a many-
sided draper, so it is better be precise with him; if
you say he is not a ‘‘ rebel ““ you may have to apolo-
gise, and if you said he was he might have you up for
slander! So let us repeat that a Union Jack—that
so grieved Murphy’s ‘‘ Independent "’--was displayed
out of a window of Murphy’s Hotel which is over
Murphy’s haberdashery.

The imitation Suffers in Treland are a farce, with a
slight bordering on possible tragedy. It is farcical
when Skeffy runs to the clergy for protection. We
_hear that he disguised . himself as a clergyman—
whether as ‘‘ Father Skeffy '’ or *“ Parson Skeffy ’ we
are not certain—and so effected entrance to the
Asquith meeting on Friday night,  As we are tempor-
arily living in a seaside resort, to which the last train
from Dublin—except on two evenings of the week—
starts at 8.20, we were unable to be present at this
meeting where Skefly took, if we may phrase it so,

THE LEADER.

Juwy 27, 1912.

benefit of clergy. - We heav that he dyed his fm.nous
whiskers, and as there is no suggestion that he
attempted to impersonate a bishop of the Church of
England’s Taithful Garrisou in ‘Ire]:\_nd, we assume
that he was driven to get out of his knickerbockers and
wear long trousers like an ordinary man for once in his
life, Ho appears to have gob off rather lightly.

The movement here is a mere imita(,iqn movcmcntr
When are men justified in rebelling against thc'lmv!
We will not go into that matter now, as to do so is not;
called for.  The plain fact is that Irishwomen are not,
by any tests thab can- be applied, ab pl'@Sunbl anxious
for the vote, certainly not anxious to press bhe)r claims
for the vote if they want it at the present juncture,
when Home Rule at the best is in dangerous waters
and needs all the help it can get to be hl‘o_ughL safely
to harbour, What the Sufters agitation is in England
we do not know, and we are not interested in it,
though we do protest against Tinglish women sending
over some of their viragoes to disturb the peace of
this law-abiding country by hatchet throwing and
attempts at firing a theatre.

We only had time to dally, on our way to catch a
train, for about three minutes in Beresford Place on
Friday evening as the imitation Suffers with consum-
mate impudence attempted to hold a public meeting.
They were just descending from the platform, under
police protection, as we had to pass on our way. We
read in the papers subsequently of the attitude of the
crowd.  Apparently Suffer baiting was taken up as a
congenial pastime during the night by some of the
class who turned out and broke windows last August.
The ‘“ Suffers > are bad enough, but no one wants last
August’s window-breaking mob to take them in hand.
Tvidently some inoffensive ladies were roughly treated
on the suspicion that they were Suffers, and no doubt
some of the underworld were glad of the opportunity
for a display of corner-boyism.. But though we con-
demn mob law—and it was low rather than what we
might call in contra distinction honest mob law at
that—the fact remains that the Suffers have rousec
the people to considerable resentments, Irishwomen
resent the gang. The modern Irish are rather a tame
people, but they are not so tame as to be intimidated
by the pack of viragoes—partly foreign—who com-
prise what ‘may be called the Skeffy gang. Who are
they?  And what right have they to speak for the
women of Ireland? Kettle, late of the gang, has
already earned the hostility of “ Parson Skeffy,” and
“ Cruso " appears to be saying or doing nothing. The
gang refrained from attempting to goad more or less
irresponsible people by refraining from the accustomed
meeting in the Pheenix Park. These viragoes have
gone too far,’and have exasperated the patient public.
They have precipitated matters to such lan extent
that their lawlessness has given an opportunity for
lawlessness on the opposite extreme. Needless to say,
we have no sympathy with the class who broke windows
last August should they take the opportunity to molest
women under cover of the public indignation at the
Skeffy gang.

Carson and his *“ provisional Government ”’ and the
patronage that the opera bouffe rebellion of ““ Ulster *’
under cerbain civcumstances at ““ no far distant date *
has given to lawless persons generally, has made
strongly for the anarchy and lawlessness that is in-
fecting many classes.  Those high-placed miscreants

" who are encouraging the poor, and in many ways
harmless, Orange dupes have a lot to answer for.

The Hairy Iairies of Plunkett House are a slippery
crowd : i is as difficult to lay an effective grip on them
as it is to hold a live cel.  The organ of pigs and
poetry, edited by the head centre of the Hairy Iairies,
published an openly abu letter to John Dillon in
the official organ of the I.A.0.8., which is yet not the
“official organ..  You become a bit topsy turvey when
you tackle the faivies; they are beyond the ordinary
tactics of ordinary mortals, We believe that if you
call the organ of pigs and poetry the official organ of
the T.A.0.5. you are met with a disclaimer. It has a
techuically sepavate existence. Somewhat similarly the
L.A.OS. and the I.A.W.S. are things apart! That
physically large but poli y void humbug, Rolleston,
though one of the gang, is a free lance when he makes
a grievous blunder.

We are independent outsiders in this matter. We
believe in co-operation as we believe in up-to-date
machinery, and we very much rvegret that co-operation
has got a serious set-back by being identified with the
Plunkett House gang and the opery hairy fairies.
We feel towards the Hairy I somewhat similarly
as genuine and common-sen vocates of votes for
some women must feel tow the Skeffy gang and
the hatchet coterie.  So long the Plunkett crowd
stand for co-operation, co-operation will be up against
a big hill—though we would be the last to say that,
because an undesirable clique have become identified

with a good cause that the cause should be sacrificed

in order to hit the undesirable people. We are not
hero-worshippers of Mr. Dillon, and few Nationalist
politicians have got more adverse attention from us
than he; we felt quite passionately against him on
* account of the attitude he took up with regard to
Bssential Irish, in which attitude he was not supported
at a great convention. We think that Mr. Dillon has
béen unduly suspicious of everything that tended to
break down—what we, as a matter of fact, did in the
end break down—the nation-killing assumption that
politics is nationality. ~ These days are gone, and no
man now remains an adherent to that foolish creed
which it was part of our business to shatter for ever.
John Dillon, pre-eminently, in our opinion, stood for
that fossilised attitude of mind, and we are willing to
agree that he looked upon the Plunkett move with
_suspicion than he probably looked upon the
Gaclic League in the early days.  But that belongs
to the past. In the course of time the Plunkett gang
took up a position, or perhaps were side-tracked into
it, which from the Irish point of view spelled disaster
for them, We probably distrust the Hairy Fairies
nearly as much now as John Dillon does, and that is
saying a great deal, though at one time so sympathetic
¢ we to them that many people convinced them-
selves that Horace Plunkett was really running the
ILLEADER, We need a man, or set of men, now who
would be competent to take the great cause of co-
operation ‘out of the hands of the hairy fairies and
place it on its merifs and place it sanely, with its possi-
bilities and its limitations, before the country. Trom
* the point of view of the earnest believer in the desira-
bility of developing co-operation in rural Ireland,
Plunkett and his Hairy Fairies are in the way as the
hatchet thrower is in the way of the cause of votes
for some women; and the best service they could do
co-operation is to step down and out.  The hairy
fairies arc so conceited and presumplious and have
- such an ‘‘ ascendancy’’ contempt for~the mere Irish

THE LEADER. 559

people, that we believe they—or some of ' them—aré.
incapable of appreciating how offensive, insolent and
impertinent they are. 2

It is not our business to interfere in the differences
between John Dillon and William G. Fairy., But the
sickening, self-righteous tone of the latter, mixed up
‘with Pill Lane abuse, will not gain any sympathy for
him or for Plunkett House. ~When it comes to a
fight the taking of sides is determined for the most part
by instinct, and the instinct will usually be justified
by the judgment of those who reflect. We are not hero-
worshippers of John Dillon, whom we recently called,
and still consider as somewhat of a political fossil, buf
when it comes to a person, such as the editor of pigs
and poetry, attacking Dillon as a landlord’s dog-boy
might attack himn, then we are on the side of Dillon.
The scramble for “ State aid’’’ for self-help, is bad
enough, but it is more bad—we suppose we may use
that phrase in those days of ‘‘less bad ’’—to keep on
the cry about the 100,000 adherents. TLet us remind
them again that at a 1s. a head these *“ self-helpers
could raise £5,000. But these hundred thousand good
people organised by the big fairies are looked upon as
a joke—if they were not a joke, if they were real,
surely they would subscribe adequately. ~But really,
we are giving too much space to the hairy fairy. Physi-
cally, and perhaps mentally, he has a marked likeness
to Skeffy.

The burning of ‘‘penny dirties  is not yet over.
The local newsagents of I'ermoy, in the Co. Cork, it
appears agreed to discontinue the sale of certain papers
to which objection was made, but within recent times
the sale of these papers had developed anew oWing to
other persons taking it up. ~We have heard of land
grabbers; what about * penny dirty '’ grabbers? On
a recent Sunday about ten o’clock, at the railway
station at Fermoy, there was an unwonted stir, The
Rosslare express was due about that time. When the
nation-killing railway train carrying dirty papers
arrived, two parcels of obnoxious papers were seized
and swiftly borne away to a field where they were
burned to ashes. If the recognised newsagents of a
town agree to discontinue the sale of certain obnoxious
papers, it is a mean thing for others not previously in
that line of business to step in and endeavour to grab
the profits that the recognised agents had given up in
the interests of decency and good morals. ¥

There were a few things in Asquith’s speech that
are worth being placed on record in these pages. He
said :—

I start then in dealing with Home Rule for Ire-
land with the proposition that Ireland is a nation
(cheers), and that the condition of the success of
any scheme that statesmanship can devise is the
recognition—the full and generous recognition—of
Irish Nationality. . ;

Again he said :— -
They talk about the Veto, and they are always
dangling it before your eyes as a kind of terrible
spectre or bogey. ~ Gentlemen, I am not in the
least afraid of the Veto. No one who'is a party
to this legislation believes that the powers of Veto
will be or can be wantonly or oppressively, or even
irritatingly put into force. ?

What is there to be done if theve is * civil war 2’
We in Treland know all about that, and can laugh at
it;; but it is onte of the bogeys held up before the simple
Englishers. ; :

Mr. Asquith remarked :—

. Certain am T that no justification could possibly
be more flimsy or frivial in character than that
which has been urged in the case we are now
considering. I am not, therefore, in the least
embarrassed when usl\ed as I constantly am:
““ What are you going to do in the event of civil
war?” T tell you qulte frankly, I do not believe
in the prospect of civil war.

We in Ireland would scarcely be so polite in our veply
to the bluster of the poor obscurant Orangemen who
think they live in the 17th century—the poor men with

¢+ timber heads and wooden muskets.  The scoundrels
* who in the excitement of a week or so were able to
terrorise Catholic workingmen from earning their bread
in-Belfast are not the material out of which wars of
any l\md are made. Waylaying defenceless workmen
and going to war are different pmposmom If a few
people in  the Belfast area don't like Home Rule, well
they will have to put up with it, as the majority of the
Irish people have had to put up with the Union for
over a century. When Home Rule comes the sensible
men will recognise the situation; a great many
agltatols will have no time to spare to talk of *‘ civil
war,’’ as all their available time will be taken up in
their efforts to get jobs; and the poor obscurant
Orangemen left to their own resources will go on earn-
ing their living in the,usual humdrum manner,

A TALK BETWEEN SWIFT AND GRATTAN.

Time—Some hours after the

Scene—College Green.
s visit.

(The statue of Grattan speaks.)

i Grattan.—The cheers have died away,

! The swelling voices of a nation’s joy

i Have died into the silence of the night,

I And College Green beneath the midnight stars
REiaa Lies calm and peaceful as a summer strand

B At ebb-tide of the sea. Oh, now at last.

- Hath England sent her harbinger of peace e
I To-speak “of rescue and redemption from
f The chains of centuries. Before mine eyes
I A vision of the future brightly spreads,
I8 And Ireland stands new-born in her strength
A nation once again,

(Enter Dean Swift.)
Swift.—Greeting, Grattan.
Grattan.—Thrice welcome, Dean.
Swift.—I saw thee shiné to-night
When Dublin went to blazes.
Grattan.—Well, Dean, thou knowest
* I am a burmng and a shining light of Iush
* nationality.
Swith. —Oh, that I grant.
. - Thou art most br uly one of hc],md s Saved.
2 Grattan.—What! Grattan a ‘“ Saved ”’ man!
- A vulgar bigot of a narrow clique !
Oh Dean, thou dost but jole.

THE LEADER. ‘o o7, 1912

Swift.—I never joke,
Tor life is far too tragic for mere fun
As T have found. hs Grattan, thou art Saved
In that thou did not gmw[ in the dirt
Of Protestant Ascendancy. Thou didst soar
Above the common ruck of stupid fools,
Who served the times with gross and carnal greed,
And deemed the mere wild Irish made to serve
Their worshipful big stomachs. So now thou hast
Found love immortal in a nation’s heart, ;
While pompous asses of the Penal age '
Are unremembered ashes.

Grattan.—Why so they are.
They might have been mere driven beasts for all
Their memories do count.

Swift.—So perish all,
Who with their donkey ]nt,dxfoll(s would attempt
To stem the rising of a nation’s tide.
Almighty God in v«,n(ml doth show
How He loves money by the sort of men
To whom He gives it. So doth He too
Arouse a nation to advance itself
By raising stupid bigots, fools and clods
To pos oracles, and to so disgust
The people to rebellion.

Grattan.—It would appear

s College founded by the good Queen Bess

s such a purpose.

Swift.—It doth. .
We must know darkness ere we prize the light,
And Treland without Trinity to show
The hatefulness of bigotry might be
Too tame to fight for freedom.

To Ireland on this night. The time may come
When it will find a very proper end
As pig or cattle market.

Turn out its bank clerks.

Before we have left July behind us we are reminded
of Christmas by the receipt of a book of samples of
Irish Christmas Cards, of new vegistered designs. The
cards are designed and printed in Ireland by Messrs.
Guy and Co., Ltd., of Cork. The ordinary reader may
think that Messrs. Guy and Co. are rafher early in
announcing their Christmas cards, but the announcing
of Irish Christmas cards so early is mainly intended for
the trade.  When it comes on near Christmas some of
the stationers may not have a supply to meet the
demands of their customers, so they had better look
into the matter now. The trade may have sample
books of forty new designs to sell at from 1d. to 6d. by
applying to Messrs. Guy and Co., Ltd., ol Cork, for
the: They should apply at once, for Irish Christmas

{ particularly when they are of such excellent
and can be sold at such cheap prices as those
Guy and Co.—are a very good selling line;

boldly on sale, and many people will clamour and even
kick up a mild row if they cannot get th-m. But unless

day to get stocks on Christmas week when the people
are buying.  Perhaps, “some of our readers who sce

trade might speak to their stationers thus early if they
think the latter need a talking.

colouring. ~ Folding cards are also to be had ab 8d.

A CHAT WITH ASQUITH.

JHEN your representative was commissioned by
nobody to interview Mr. Asquith on the
occasion of his visit to Dublin last week, naturally
he felt honoured at the important duty assigned to
him. Interviews of this character are always interest-
ing—to the interviewer. I betook myself, therefore,
in due course to the Theatre Royal where Mr. Asquith
was appearing, and inquived whether T might have the
pleasure of secing him.  The Customs officers—I
mean, of course, the officials on duty at the theatre,
but one gets a bit mixed nowadays when prominent
actors on the world’s stage find themselves in Hippo-
dromes and the like—were stern and forbidding. In-
deed, they not only forbade, they commanded. No
doubt should we secure control of the Customs under
Home Rule they will behave better. TFortunately I
had nothing to declave.  After I had been searched
for gunpowder, fusil oil, and petroleum, and was found
guiltless of these contraband articles, my ticket, that
is to say my card, was at last accepted and sent in.
Matches, which an eagle-eyed official detected in one
of my pockets, were confiscated, but as they were
Irish-made and I pleaded the cigarette-habit, they
were not regarded as indicative of incendiary intent.

BELFAST QUEEN’S AND IKISH.

(13 ULSTER > in all its ramifications is playing with
the unison and precision of an orchestra—
playing into Ireland’s hands.  When, some months
ago, the Senate of Queen’s University, yielding to the
repeated représentations of the local Gaelic League,
consented to raise its Irish Lectureship to the status
of a Chair, some of.ns wondered was the news too good
to be true. And of course it was. Or at least, it was
too good to remain true. TLast week the Chair was,
figuratively speaking, taken and publicly burned by the
common hangman of the institution, and Irish in
Queen'’s is once more down in the ha’penny place.
The story, however, deserves o be set out in greater
detail. On the establishment referred to of the Chair,
Irish Ireland was, of course, delighted. It applauded
the Queen’s. The applause seems to have awaked the
** Ulster ” demon in the University and, on awaking,
he seems to have seen that it was a case of ‘‘ ordering
up his last reserves his latest chance to try.”” He
sounded the alarm and the reserves came dutifully to
heel. Boldly they met and boldly they rescinded the
former resolution. Bold, however, as they were, they
thought some explanation of their action due to the
public. The explanation was forthcoming—the funds
at the disposal of the University were not sufficient to
pay for the Irish Chair.  That, mind you, after the
question of funds had been fully gone into before the
Chair was voted. ~We of course didn’t believe the
tale, but some of us pretended we did. We called a
meeting.  The meeting came {ogether and resolved
that ‘‘ this meeting of citizens of Belfast * offer the
Senate of Queen’s £1,000, that being the difference
between the expense of the existing Irish Lectureship
and that of the abolished Chair during the four years
yet to elapse of the present Senate’s term of office—
the whole question to be reconsidered at the end of the
four years. Well, the £1,000 was handed in by our
deputation ‘last weel, and the result was what I have
stated in my first paragraph. In official parlance the
Senate could not see its way to re-open the question.
I should say that personally I was all along inclined
to see the humorous side‘of the Irish plan of campaign.
I could see a lot of good uscs to which to put £1,000
besides giving it to the bigots of Queen’s. The
speakers, however, at the citizen’s meeting that voted
the money seemed to be in solemn earnest. The Pro-

What, meanwhile, is the immediate effect as regards
the Irish language? Well, £1,000 has been, in a sense,

“Conal Ceapnac” the money, but it was all along.
only a lying excuse that Queen’s couldn’t afford the
money itself.  Then again, not only have the bigots
been, as I have said, unmasked, but the unmasking
has been effected at the psychological moment. By
the accident of the Chair question having cropped up
at this time of political stress, the Irish language has
been raised up once more before the world and shown
to be part and parcel of that Ireland a Nation at which
“‘ Ulster "* is pelting paving stones in the streets and
spitting in high places. Lastly, the fact has been
demonstrated that, as our editor has said of the fool,
the more you educate a bigot the more bigotted you
make him. Personally I think we would have had no
University Essential Irish difficulty if only we had had

course would be to send yet another deputation to the
Senate, They have, amongst other things, made
themselves so ridiculous that they may see no further
comicality in re-establishing the Irish Chair. In any
case, the matter and the Senate must be kept before
the public. The elections to Senates and other things
under Home Rule are in reality being fought out now,
and it is desirable to keep Lord Shaftesbury and his
